solutions.solution: Again, you are looking for an alternative causal explanation for the paradoxical result that there was no increase in the incidence of rape, but an increase in the number of cases being decided by the courts. One such alternative explanation is that you can draw a further inference from the information that courts were not lenient with rape cases, but that they used strict ornament, and therefore They wanted simple, light fixes. Then, the courts' stance that was one common way of disposition of this attitude. As exemplified by current exhibition. We may infer from this that there was a strong commitment to investigating the function of ornamentation.

This is one of those items that ask you to find one alternative explanation. Here, you are asked to find the one choice that does not provide an alternative explanation that would help eliminate the paradox. Every choice but (D) suggests a way of eliminating the paradox. (D), however, if anything, strengthens the paradox instead of providing any way of explanation away the paradox.

Stronger patent laws increase protection; protection encourages investment and labour productivity. Thus, stronger patent laws initiate a chain of events that often culminates in improved productivity. Choice C expresses that and is, therefore, the best answer. Choice A is inappropriate because its logic, if any, that financial institutions would play in investment is left open. The increased productivity mentioned in B may mean fewer hours of labour for a given level of output, and may, thus, threaten jobs. Investments of the sort described in C may already be at the most possible level. The passage given indicates that law reform. Hence, there is no attempt to isolate the causes of economic recession.

If many copper was sold that goods would and some sales must have come from surplus stocks, and those stocks and that they dropped during the three year period in question, so, D is the best answer. Choice A is inappropriate because the information in the passage is consistent with pepper being a profitable crop if grown on a modest scale. The passage provides us figures on which to base estimates of production and consumption of pepper, such as those made in B. Choice C is inconsistent with the information in the passage that states that world acreage has to return to previous levels unless either the pepper price increases are to go up and it reflects remaining average. No clear inferences can be made about the growth of profits in the last three years.
The ecologically illiterate among the young people cannot make informed decisions about ecological issues that are increasingly important. Therefore Choice A can be inferred and is the best answer.

Evaluating Inferences. Choices B, C and D are inappropriate because while the passage permits a problem and allows the general outlines of a solution to be inferred, specific solutions such as those presented in B and C go beyond what can be inferred, as do recommendations like D that address potential problems that are not touched on in the passage. Choice E is too specific, and suggests supports the conclusion that some young people will be unable to make informed decisions, but it suggests nothing about the tasks of those young people which make.

If, as the stimulus states, the electorate is split evenly between two parties, and then a third-party candidate attracts votes from only one of the two parties, even the party that loses no votes to the third party is unlikely to capture more than one-half of the votes. Therefore, B is the best answer. Choice A is inappropriate because it contradicts information given in the passage, while C goes beyond the passage, which does not exclude new voters from those 'who might otherwise have voted for one of the two major candidates'. The information given in the passage is not specific enough to make the inferences about the politics, or the founding, of the third party, as suggested in D and E.

Africa-first continentalism is characteristic of a continent with many tribes that are ethnically different. Statement (B) is flawed; Angola and Ethiopia are not given as worst-case examples. Alternatives (D) and (E) are not true. Alternative (C) is the second best summarisation, even though inferior to it most inferred.

Alternative (D) cannot be implied from the statement. There is no information in the statement that implies that wives earn more than husbands. Alternative (A) may be implied because as wives contributed to the household nominal income, if prices increased at the same rate as the income, real income would stay the same. Alternatives (B) and (C) are implicit in the statement. If the added income contribution of wives leaves average family income at the level it was 20 years ago, then the reason must be that the price level was lower 20 years ago. So, (E) is implied.

Choice (A) is vague, while (D) equates services trade with legislation. Be sure (C) is irrelevant to the argument. (E) comes close to summarising the argument but it is incomplete. Un-co-ordinated is not an acronym for criticism.

The statement's conclusion is that all towns have unsavoury characters. This conclusion is false. According to the passage, only towns with pool halls have unsavoury characters and since we cannot infer that all towns have pool halls, conclusion (C) is wrong. Alternatives (A) and (B) are stated in the passage, while alternatives (D) and (E) can be deduced.
